
## Log-rank statistic (two groups)

For two groups ($G = 2$), the observed-minus-expected scores satisfy
$O_1 - E_1 = -(O_2 - E_2)$ (the total failures at each $t_i$ are by
definition partitioned across the two groups), so it does not matter
which group we use to form the test statistic.

:::{#thm-logrank-two-group}
#### Log-rank statistic, two groups

For either $g \in \{1, 2\}$,

$$
\chi^2_{\text{LR}}
\;=\; \frac{(O_g - E_g)^2}{\widehat{\Varf{O_g - E_g}}}
\;\stackrel{H_0}{\sim}\; \chi^2_1
$$ {#eq-logrank-stat-2grp}

where $r_{1i}$ and $r_{2i}$ are the at-risk counts for groups 1 and 2
at time $t_i$ (i.e., $r_{gi}$ from @sec-logrank-notation for $g = 1, 2$),
and the variance estimate is

$$
\widehat{\Varf{O_g - E_g}}
\;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{k}
   \frac{r_{1i}\, r_{2i}\, d_i\, (r_i - d_i)}{r_i^2\,(r_i - 1)}.
$$ {#eq-logrank-var-2grp}

For two groups, the variance is the same whether computed for group 1 or
group 2.

[@kleinbaum2012survival, pp. 69--70, two-group variance formula]

:::

::: notes
@eq-logrank-var-2grp is the hypergeometric variance for the count of
failures from group 1 at $t_i$ conditional on $(d_i, r_{1i}, r_{2i})$,
summed over failure times. Each summand has the form
$r_{1i} r_{2i} d_i (r_i - d_i) / [r_i^2 (r_i - 1)]$.
:::
